title: Electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers: from planar honeycomb to the puckered phase
authors: Niloufar Dadkhah, Walter R. L. Lambrecht
abstract: Group-V monolayer materials exhibit intriguing electronic and optical properties, influenced by their unique crystal symmetries and structural phases. In this work, we study arsenic monolayers, investigating their electronic and optical properties across different phases, including planar, and puckered forms, using density functional theory (DFT) and quasi-particle self-consistent $GW$ (QS$GW$) methods, with and without vertex contributions (ladder diagrams) and examine the effects of spin-orbit coupling and the orbital composition of the bands. The Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) method is used to study the optical response and the band origin of the low lying excitons is determined. The gradual transformation from the puckered $α$-phase to the flat honeycomb structure is studied under biaxial strain and the evolution of the band structure and optical response is described in terms of band inversions of bands of different orbital character.
summary: This paper explores how the electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers change when they are flattened by biaxial strain. Using advanced many-body methods, the authors show that while simple models predict a direct gap, more accurate calculations reveal an indirect gap and significant orbital hybridization. They also demonstrate that strain can turn bright excitons into dark ones, providing insight into the tunable optoelectronic properties of 2D materials.
main_problem: The study investigates the evolution of electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers as they undergo a structural transition from a puckered alpha-phase to a flat honeycomb phase under biaxial strain.
main_result: The researchers found that quasiparticle corrections reveal an indirect band gap and significant p-d orbital hybridization, and that biaxial strain induces a transition where the lowest exciton shifts from bright to dark.
method: The study uses Density Functional Theory (DFT) with LDA, many-body perturbation theory via the quasi-particle self-consistent GW (QSGW) and QSW methods, and the Bethe-Salpeter Equation (BSE) for optical response.
model_or_system: The physical system is a monolayer of arsenic (As), a Group-V element, studied in two structural phases: a puckered alpha-phase (similar to black phosphorus) and a flat honeycomb structure.
key_observables: Band gaps (direct and indirect), orbital composition (p and d character), exciton binding energies, optical anisotropy (x vs y polarization), and exciton brightness.
important_parameters: Biaxial strain levels, spin-orbit coupling (SOC), and in-plane/out-of-plane bond lengths and angles.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of the LDA and QSGW approximations for electronic structure and the BSE for electron-hole interactions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure and Brillouin zone; Figure 2 compares LDA, QSGW, and QSW band structures; Figure 3 shows SOC effects; Figure 4 shows orbital-resolved bands; Figure 5 compares dielectric functions (IPA vs BSE); Figures 6-7 show k-space distribution of excitons; Figures 10-13 show band structure evolution under strain and Brillouin zone folding.
paper_structure: The paper begins with structural characterization, moves to electronic structure calculations (LDA, QSGW, and SOC effects), analyzes orbital hybridization, investigates optical properties and excitonic effects, and concludes with a study of the phase transition under biaxial strain.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Crystal structure of α-arsenene shown from the top view (a) and the side view (c), along with the correspond- ing reciprocal-space Brillouin zone and high-symmetry points (b). In panels (a) and (c), the green arrows and dashed line indicate a glide-mirror symmetry operation. The operation consists of a non-primitive translation followed by a mirror reflection, where the mirror plane passes halfway between the two sublattices, as illustrated by the dashed line in (c). The Cartesian axes are defined as x ∥a, y ∥b, and z ∥c, where y corresponds to the puckering direction and x lies along the shorter in-plane lattice vector.
FIG. 2. Band structure of the relaxed puckered phase calculated using (a) LDA, (b) QSGW, and (c) QSG ˆ W. The y-axis shows energy (eV), and the x-axis follows the chosen high-symmetry path.

Fig. 2 shows the band structure of the relaxed puckered phase obtained using LDA, QSGW, and QSG ˆW. For the DFT starting point, structural parameters were taken from the Materials Project [24, 25] and further relaxed using QE. The in-plane lattice constants are a = 3.69 ˚A and b = 4.72 ˚A, and a vacuum spacing of 22.5 ˚A was used along the out-of-plane direction to avoid interac- tions between periodic images. In LDA, the gap is direct at an intermediate point between Γ and Y. After includ- ing quasiparticle corrections, both QSGW and QSG ˆW predict an indirect gap, with the VBM located between Γ and Y and the CBM at Γ. The lowest conduction band also displays shallow local minima along Γ–X...

FIG. 3. Band structure of α-arsenene including SOC. Spin– orbit coupling mixes the spin character of the bands; blue and red colors indicate spin-up and spin-down contributions, respectively, with intermediate colors corresponding to mixed character.
FIG. 4. Orbital-resolved band structures from (a) LDA and (b) QSGW calculations. Colors denote s (blue), p (green), and d (red) character.

Fig. 5 shows the imaginary part of the macroscopic dielectric function for light polarized along the two in- plane directions. The independent-particle approxima- tion (IPA) spectrum is compared with the corresponding BSE result, where electron–hole interactions and local- field effects are explicitly included. As expected for the puckered structure, the optical response is strongly anisotropic: the x and y polarizations show different onset energies and different distributions of oscillator strength. In both polarizations the BSE spectrum is red- shifted relative to the IPA result, reflecting the binding of electron–hole pairs. The first bright exciton appears near 1.8 eV for x...
FIG. 6. k-space distributions of the first two bright excitons with y polarization. (a) First exciton, at 1.089 eV, mainly near Γ. (b) Second exciton, at 1.175 eV, concentrated along the Γ–Y path.

FIG. 5. Imaginary part of the macroscopic dielectric func- tion for (a) x-polarized and (b) y-polarized light. Orange: independent-particle approximation (IPA). Blue: Bethe– Salpeter equation (BSE). The first and second bright excitons are labeled as 1 and 2, respectively.

FIG. 7. k-space distributions of the first two bright excitons for x polarization. (a) Lowest bright exciton at 1.787 eV. (b) Second bright exciton, at 1.988 eV, plotted with weights scaled by 100 for visibility.

FIG. 8. Guide to the structural parameters that vary under strain, as shown in Fig. 9. Orange circles indicate (a) the in-plane bond length d1, (b) the out-of-plane bond length d2, (c) the in-plane bond angle θ1, and (d) the out-of-plane bond angle θ2. Upon going from the relaxed puckered structure to the flat limit, d1 changes from 2.509 to 2.521 ˚A, d2 from 2.490 to 2.523 ˚A, θ1 from 94.783◦to 120.057◦, and θ2 from 100.367◦
